coredata.dc:description: Considering functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) are potential to be applied in many areas, their risk and toxicity to organisms are imperative to be evaluated. Thus, this study conducted acute toxicity test of zebrafish (Danio rerio) at embryonic and adult stage to estimate the toxicity of FCNs. Results show that the toxic effects of FCNs and nitrogen doped FCNs (N-FCNs) at their 10% lethal concentration (LC10) values on zebrafish are expressed in developmental retardation, cardiovascular toxicity, renal damage and hepatotoxicity. There are interactive relationships between these effects, but the main reason should be ascribed to the undesirable oxidative damage induced by high doses of materials, as well as the biodistribution of FCNs and N-FCNs in vivo. Even so, FCNs and N-FCNs can promote the antioxidant activity in zebrafish tissues to cope with the oxidative stress. FCNs and N-FCNs are not easy to cross the physical barriers in zebrafish embryos or larvae, and can be excreted from intestine by adult fish, which proves their biosecurity to zebrafish. In addition, because of the differences in physicochemical properties, especially nano-size and surface chemical property, FCNs show higher biosecurity to zebrafish than N-FCNs. The effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on hatching rates, mortality rates and developmental malformations are dose-dependent and time-dependent. The LC50 values of FCNs and N-FCNs on zebrafish embryo at 96 hpf are 1610 mg/L and 649 mg/L, respectively. According to the Acute Toxicity Rating Scale of the Fish and Wildlife Service, the toxicity grades of FCNs and N-FCNs are both defined as “practically nontoxic”, and FCNs are “Relatively Harmless” to embryos because their LC50 values are above 1000 mg/L. Our results prove the biosecurity of FCNs-based materials for future practical application.

FCNs and N-FCNs induced lethality curves to embryos. a, lethality curve of FCNs at 96 hpf. b, lethality curve of N-FCNs at 96 hpf. c, lethality curve of FCNs at 120 hpf. d, lethality curve of N-FCNs at 120 hpf. N = 3.

Hatching rate of zebrafish embryos exposed to FCNs or N-FCNs at different concentrations. a, 50 mg/L FCNs or N-FCNs exposure. b, FCNs or N-FCNs at LC10 value (516 mg/L and 197 mg/L, respectively). *: P < 0.1. **: P < 0.05. ***: P < 0.01. N = 3.

Morphological effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on zebrafish embryos. a, Malformation rate of embryos at 72 hpf. b-d, Typical malformations after exposure to FCNs or N-FCNs at 72 hpf (E: eye. H: heart. L: liver. In: intestines. YSD: yolk sac diameter.). Bar = 200 μm. e, Heart rate of embryos at 72 hpf. f, Body length of embryos at 72 hpf. Lowercase letters indicate the significant differences (P < 0.05). N = 3.
MDA content (a) and T-AOC (b) of zebrafish larvae in different treatment. *: P < 0.1. **: P < 0.05. ***: P < 0.01. N = 3.
MDA content (a), and SOD (b), POD (c) and CAT (d) activity in liver and gill of adult zebrafish exposed to FCNs or N-FCNs at 50 mg/L *: P < 0.1. **: P < 0.05. ***: P < 0.01. N = 3.

10

Lowercase letters indicate the significant differences (P < 0.05). N = 3. head.ce:title: Ecotoxicological evaluation of functional carbon nanodots using zebrafish (Danio rerio) model at different developmental stages

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) are potential to be applied in many areas, their risk and toxicity to organisms are imperative to be evaluated. Thus, this study conducted acute toxicity test of zebrafish (Danio rerio) at embryonic and adult stage to estimate the toxicity of FCNs. Results show that the toxic effects of FCNs and nitrogen doped FCNs (N-FCNs) at their 10% lethal concentration (LC10) values on zebrafish are expressed in developmental retardation, cardiovascular toxicity, renal damage and hepatotoxicity. There are interactive relationships between these effects, but the main reason should be ascribed to the undesirable oxidative damage induced by high doses of materials, as well as the biodistribution of FCNs and N-FCNs in vivo. Even so, FCNs and N-FCNs can promote the antioxidant activity in zebrafish tissues to cope with the oxidative stress. FCNs and N-FCNs are not easy to cross the physical barriers in zebrafish embryos or larvae, and can be excreted from intestine by adult fish, which proves their biosecurity to zebrafish. In addition, because of the differences in physicochemical properties, especially nano-size and surface chemical property, FCNs show higher biosecurity to zebrafish than N-FCNs. The effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on hatching rates, mortality rates and developmental malformations are dose-dependent and time-dependent. The LC50 values of FCNs and N-FCNs on zebrafish embryo at 96 hpf are 1610 mg/L and 649 mg/L, respectively. According to the Acute Toxicity Rating Scale of the Fish and Wildlife Service, the toxicity grades of FCNs and N-FCNs are both defined as “practically nontoxic”, and FCNs are “Relatively Harmless” to embryos because their LC50 values are above 1000 mg/L. Our results prove the biosecurity of FCNs-based materials for future practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Qiong Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft; Biao Yan: Formal analysis, Software; Xiufeng Cao: Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing, Funding acquisition; Zhijiang Guo: Validation, Resources; Zhenjie Xi: Data Curation, Formal analysis; Jianwei Li: Software, Formal analysis; Naixuan Ci: Methodology; Mei Yan: Resources, Funding acquisition; Lijie Ci: Project administration, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

Carbon nanodots (CDs), a rising star in carbon nanomaterials, have attracted eno89rmous attention in many research fields due to their outstanding properties including low cytotoxicity, good water dispersibility, superior biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and catalytic properties (He et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). Therefore, CDs have been widely applied in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, catalysis, as well as environmental remediation and monitoring (Long et al., 2021; He et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2021a; Liu et al., 2021). Considering their promising applications, it is imperative to evaluate the risk and toxicity of CDs at the early stage of research development to minimize undesirable impacts on human and environmental health (de Medeiros et al., 2021).

The acute toxicity test of zebrafish (Danio rerio) are often used for assessing environmental or ecological hazards of chemicals and environmental pollutants, given their high genetic homology to humans, their rapid reproduction and development, their small size, and their transparency during early development and growth stages (Xiong et al., 2022). In recent years, zebrafish models have been also used to evaluate the ecotoxicity of nanoparticles (NPs), including gold NPs (Ginzburg et al., 2018), silver NPs (Zhao et al., 2022), graphene (Neri et al., 2022), graphene oxide (Clemente et al., 2019), nano-ZnO (Hou et al., 2019), microplastics (Li et al., 2020), quantum dots (Deng et al., 2022), carbon nanotubes (Martinez et al., 2019), etc. The reported studies about the toxicology of CDs are mainly focused on their neurotoxicity, gene expression and fluorescence effect at narrow exposure dose and time range (Zhao et al., 2022; Hu et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2020). However, relatively limited researches were conducted to systematically reveal the changes in the development, antioxidant activity and histopathology of zebrafish at different life stages exposed to various doses of CDs. What's more, the ecotoxicity of CDs is influenced by their physicochemical properties (including pH, size, composition, functional groups, surface charge, etc.), spatial dispersity, exposure route, dose-response, and exposure time (Falinski et al., 2019; Clemente et al., 2019). To realize their commercial potential, it is essential to fully understand how the structures and properties influence the functionality of nanomaterials, not just desired positive performance, but also undesired negative implications (Falinski et al., 2019).

To be noted, the precursor and method could influence the physicochemical properties of CDs, thus resulting in different toxicological responses to organism (Liu et al., 2020). In our previous research, functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) and nitrogen-doped FCNs (N-FCNs) were synthesized via a green and cheap electrolysis process, which showed universal bio-safety and biocompatibility to plants and soil microbe under normal or stress condition (Chen et al., 2020a, 2021a, 2021b, 2022b). As FCNs and N-FCNs have potential applications in agricultural and environmental field, there is an urgent need to systematically evaluate the nanotoxicity of these materials, as well as the influence of the shape, size, and surface chemistry on their biosafety.

In this work, zebrafish model is applied to evaluate the ecological toxicity of FCNs and N-FCNs. Their toxicity to zebrafish was assessed at different developmental stages including embryo, larvae and adult. Hatching rate, mortality, malformation rate, apoptosis and histopathology were analyzed to assess the influence of FCNs and N-FCNs on zebrafish development. The biodistribution and antioxidant system in larvae and adult zebrafish influenced by FCNs and N-FCNs were also explored. Finally, the differences in the ecotoxicity of FCNs and N-FCNs to zebrafish were systematically explored from the perspective of physiological, biochemical and physiochemical aspects. It will provide a theoretical basis and data support for the ecological toxicity evaluation of FCNs-based materials, and a more accurate reference standard to determine their safety limit, which is significant for the further practical application of the materials.

FCNs and N-FCNs were synthesized according to our previous reported electrolysis method (Chen et al., 2021a), which is described in supplementary materials (SMs). Morphology, surface chemistry and optical properties of the materials were characterized using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-1011, Japan), Raman spectrometer (inVia, Renishaw, Britain), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 37, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, America), Malvin laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Britain) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS, Edinburgh FLS980, Britain).

The animal experiment of this study was relegated to Hangzhou Hunter Biotechnology, Inc., China, and approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (Approval number: 2021-3220-01). Acute toxicity test of FCNs and N-FCNs to embryos were carried out as described in the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) guidelines (OECD, 2013). Zebrafish (wide-type AB) at about 6 months old were maintained for spawning. Adult male and female zebrafish were kept separately at constant temperature (28 °C) in a 14-h light/10-h dark cycle. Water conductivity was approximately 500 μS/cm, pH was from 6.5 to 8.5, hardness was 50–100 mg/L CaCO3, dissolved oxygen content was above 6.0 mg/L. Fish were fed twice daily with brine shrimps (Artemia nauplii). Spawning process is described detailly in SMs (Section S1.2). The newly-laid eggs for embryo acute toxicity test were collected at 6 h post fertilization (hpf). Commercially available red zebrafish of about 3-month-old were used for acute toxicity test of adult zebrafish, which were also cultured as above condition.

Embryo toxicity assays. Zebrafish embryos at 6 hpf were randomly transferred into 6-well plates, with 30 individuals per well containing 10 ml test solution (biological parallel N = 3). FCNs and N-FCNs concentrations in test solution were set as 0 (control, groups as CT), 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 mg/L, according to the predicted concentrations of nanomaterials in aquatic ecosystems (Martínez-Álvarez et al., 2021). Development indices, including mortality rates, hatching rates, malformation rates, heart rates and body length (from head to tail), were recorded daily during the entire experiment using a dissecting microscope (OLYMPUS SZX7, Japan). Mortality rates at 96 h and 120 h were used to calculate the 50% lethal concentration (LC50) values by plotting dose response curves between percentage mortality and concentrations.

Toxicity of FCNs or N-FCNs in adult zebrafish. Zebrafish at about 3 months were used for evaluating the toxicity and biodistribution of FCNs and N-FCNs in adult zebrafish. As some samples were more toxic to adult fish, the concentrations of FCNs and N-FCNs used in adult fish acute toxicity test were set as 0, 100, 300 and 500 mg/L (N = 4). To ensure the results of such studies maintain relevance to human systems, the longer-term study of 7 days was employed (Munro et al., 2008).
Experimental details of this part are supplied in the SMs (Section S1.3).

During exposure, thirty zebrafish embryos or larvae were examined for hatching rate, mortality, morphological changes, heart rate and body length using microscope attached to a camera (VertA1, China). The hatching rate was calculated as the percentage of the hatched embryos of all the living embryos at 24, 48, 72 and 96 hpf (Bhagat et al., 2021). The mortality of embryos/larvae exposed to various dosages (from 50 to 800 mg/L) of FCNs/N-FCNs was calculated at 96 and 120 hpf. The phenotypes of embryos/larvae at each developmental stage were photographed at 24, 48 and 72 hpf. The rate of malformation, including growth retardation, organs malformation and body deformity, were observed and calculated at 72 hpf. The experiments were conducted in triplicates.

The total antioxidant capacity (T-AOC) and malondialdehyde (MDA) content of larvae at 120 hpf, as well as MDA content and enzymatic activity of superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) in adult zebrafish were measured by the commercial test kit (Beijing Coolaber Technology Co., LTD, China) in triplicates. Apoptosis in zebrafish larvae at 120 hpf was studied by TUNEL staining (Lin et al., 2022). The measures were described in detail in the SMs (Section S1.3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tracing of FCNs and N-FCNs
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs or N-FCNs exposed zebrafish embryos at 7 hpf and larvae at 5 dpf were observed under UV light excitation of 340 nm by a stereomicroscope (Nikon AZ100, Japan). To avoid the adverse effects of UV light on organisms, embryos were scanned by UV light for less than 300 ms (Deng et al., 2019). The chorion of fertilized ovum in each treatment was detected by TEM. Adult zebrafish at 96 h exposure were anesthetized, and then FCNs or N-FCNs in gills and liver were measured using Raman spectrometer. The faeces of adult fish were collected and dried, then detected by Raman spectrometer.

Zebrafish larvae at 120 hpf were treated by hypothermic anesthesia. The gill, liver and kidney were collected and fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde for 48 h, then subjected to hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. Histopathological analysis was conducted via dissecting microscope.

The statistical analysis was performed by SPSS 26.0. Data were statistically analyzed via Tukey's test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with the least significance difference (LSD) test. Significant differences are indicated by lowercase letters or * (P < 0.1), ** (P < 0.05) and *** (P < 0.01). Data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD).

The well soluble FCNs and N-FCNs were obtained by the green electrolysis method with no reagent loss or further purification, which is effective and environmentally friendly. TEM results in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S1 show that FCNs and N-FCNs used in this study are carbon-based nanodots of 2–7 nm and 1–7 nm, respectively. Both FCNs and N-FCNs are easily dissolved in water, and the pH of 1.0 g/L solution is about 2.0 and 6.8, respectively. The zeta potentials of FCNs and N-FCNs at pH 7.0 are −28.9 and −20.1 eV (Fig. S2), reflecting the surfaces of the two materials are both electronegative under husbandry condition, which is corresponding to the previous report (Chen et al., 2022a). From the Raman spectra in Fig. 1b, FCNs have higher degree of disorder than N-FCNs according to the higher I
D/G value, which might be caused by the more abundant organic functional groups on FCNs. By calculation from XPS survey spectra (Fig. 1d and e), the relative atom content of oxygen to carbon is 1.6: 1 in FCNs, and the relative atom content of O: N: C is 0.55: 0.12: 1. FTIR (Fig. 1c and Table S1) and XPS spectra (Fig. 1f–h) of lyophilized FCNs and N-FCNs show that the main functional groups of FCNs are –COOH, –COOR, –OH, OC-R, C–O–C, etc., while N-FCNs have groups including –OH, NH4 +, –NH2, C–N, -COO-, OC-R, C–O–C, etc. Therefore, FCNs and N-FCNs are carbon-based nanodots with high content of electronegative functional groups, which endow them green fluorescence under 363 nm excitation wavelength. The fluorescence properties of FCNs and N-FCNs materials allow them to be detected in situ in vivo of organism (Wei et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acute toxicity of FCNs and N-FCNs to zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Zebrafish at embryonic and adult periods were used to assess the toxic effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on organisms. Lethality curves to embryos and adult zebrafish induced by FCNs or N-FCNs are presented in Fig. 2 , Fig. S3 and Fig. S4, which follow a dose-dependent and time-dependent manner. That is, high dose of NPs and long-term exposure cause more serious toxic effects to zebrafish. The LC50 values of FCNs and N-FCNs on zebrafish embryo at 96 hpf are 1610 mg/L and 649 mg/L, respectively, which are much higher than the values at 120 hpf. This also suggests that longer exposure of FCNs or N-FCNs results in greater toxicity to embryos. The LC50 values of FCNs and N-FCNs to adult fish at 96 h are 822 and 201 mg/L (Figs. S3 and S4), respectively, indicating the samples are more poison to adult fish.

Compared with adults, zebrafish embryos and larvae were less sensitive to FCNs or N-FCNs, probably because: Firstly, embryos and larvae always sink in the bottom of the water layer, which greatly declines their contact with the nanoparticles (Kimmel and Law, 1985; Najafian et al., 2014). Secondly, due to the protective role of chorion as the barrier against NPs in water, embryos are more tolerance to external toxins, such as NPs (Chen et al., 2015). Thirdly, larvae absorb toxin in water mainly via skin, and this inefficient passive absorption makes it difficult to transport nanoparticles into the internal body (Pan et al., 2021).

Furthermore, FCNs show higher biosecurity than N-FCNs due to the difference in their physiochemical properties. First, FCNs with larger size (Fig. S1) are less toxic than N-FCNs, mainly depending on the particle size inducing factors including contact probability, accumulation capability, release profiles and subsequent transmission efficiency (Huang et al., 2022). Second, surface chemical properties such as groups and charges of NPs drive their toxicity (Martínez-Álvarez et al., 2021; Falinski et al., 2019). On one hand, the high concentration of ammonium in N-FCNs solution can cause severe toxicity to zebrafish, and amino can change the molecular structure of DNA (Huang et al., 2022). On the other hand, FCNs with more negative charges are more easily to be electrostatically repulsed by the negatively charged chorion surface or membrane, leading to better biosecurity (Falinski et al., 2019).

Although FCNs and N-FCNs at lethal dosages may cause undesired toxicity to zebrafish, the toxicity grades of both materials could be defined as “practically nontoxic” owing to the higher LC50 values than 100 mg/L, according to the Acute Toxicity Rating Scale of the Fish and Wildlife Service in Table S2 (El-Harbawi, 2014). Indeed, FCNs are “Relatively Harmless” to embryos because the LC50 is above 1000 mg/L (El-Harbawi, 2014). Together, these results highlighted the biosecurity of FCNs and N-FCNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on zebrafish development at early stage

ce:section.ce:para: The mortality of embryos exposed to FCNs or N-FCNs increases in a dose- and time-dependent manner (Fig. 2 and S5). FCNs and N-FCNs samples, especially N-FCNs, cause higher mortality to embryos after 120 h of exposure than that after 96 h of exposure. Although the hatching rate of surviving embryos in each treatment is significantly different at 24 hpf and 48 hpf, but not after 72 hpf (Fig. 3 ). At 48 hpf, both of FCNs and N-FCNs at LC10 concentration dramatically inhibit embryos hatching in Fig. 3b. After 72 hpf, the hatching rates between three treatment show insignificant differences. However, when the concentration of samples decreased to 50 mg/L, only FCNs decrease the hatching rate of zebrafish embryos, and which only happens at 24 and 48 hpf. Surprisingly, 50 mg/L of N-FCNs significantly promote embryos hatching within 48 hpf, as well as 50 mg/L of FCNs or N-FCNs at 72 hpf.

Low doses of FCNs and N-FCNs can promote embryos hatching of zebrafish, the reasons are speculated as: (1), Low doses of contaminants can stimulate reproduction, growth, and behavior of organisms by hormesis (Rix et al., 2022). (2), Hatching efficiency depends on the number of chorion pore canals (CPC) (Shih et al., 2016). The zebrafish chorion contains several canals of approximately 0.6–0.7 μm for transporting oxygen and nutrients to ensure embryo growth and development (Kim and Tanguay, 2014). At the early stage of exposure, the NPs mixed with soft flocs (including secretion, impurities, etc.) agglomerate on the chorion surface, thus block the CPC and lower the activity of zebrafish eggs (de Medeiros et al., 2021b), which leads to the decrease of hatching in FCNs treated groups within 48 h. However, once FCNs or N-FCNs are distributed on the chorion surface to form a rigid film, the increase in effective particle size (hydrodynamic diameter) will lead to the high porosity of the surface on the NPs layer. Thus, the accumulation of oxygen and nutrients in the vicinity of “rigid film” can promote embryos hatching under low doses of FCNs or N-FCNs exposure (Shih et al., 2016). (3), The side effects of N-FCNs on antioxidant enzyme also play a promotion role, as the appropriate concentration of H2O2 can induce the gene expression related to development, reproduction and regeneration in zebrafish (Deng et al., 2019). Therefore, the promotion effect of N-FCNs on embryo hatching is more significant than FCNs.

However, high doses (LC10 dose in Fig. 3b) of FCNs and N-FCNs show adverse impact on zebrafish hatching, especially within 48 h, probably because: On one hand, FCNs and N-FCNs at high concentration are more easily to agglomerate and accumulate, thus seal or block the chorion pores and inhibit the transport and supply of oxygen and nutrients (Cong et al., 2017). On the other hand, FCNs and N-FCNs within 10 nm can penetrate chorion through CPCs and accumulate inside the embryo as early as 24 hpf, then trigger a series of adverse reactions in the embryo (Pitt et al., 2018). Therefore, FCNs and N-FCNs exposure at high concentrations cause decrease in the hatching rate and survival rate of zebrafish embryo.

From Fig. 4 a-c
and Figs. S7–8, typical developmental malformations of zebrafish embryos are observed during 72 h of exposure to FCNs or N-FCNs at LC10 concentration, including pericardial edema (PE), decreased blood flow (BF) and heart rate (HR), lack of liver, yolk sac malformation (YSM), renal edema (RE), and decreased body length. No other malformations are found in brain, underjaw, eyes, intestines, notochord, muscle and body color, as well as hemorrhage or thrombus. The adverse effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on the early development of embryos and larvae are dose- and time- dependent, as higher levels of materials with longer exposure time induce more serious malformations including developmental retardation, PE, absorption delay of yolk sac, and tail bending (Figs. S7–8). Both of FCNs and N-FCNs at LC10 level induce decreased BF, YSM and decreased body length in 100% zebrafish embryos. Besides, LC10 of FCNs exposure results in lack of liver and RE in 36% and 34% embryos, respectively, while LC10 of N-FCNs cause serious PE in 29% embryos (Fig. 4c). FCNs and N-FCNs can significantly decline heart rates and body length of zebrafish embryos, especially N-FCNs, and which are dose-dependent (Fig. 4d–f).

The malformations in liver, kidney, YSD and body length show the developmental toxicity of FCNs and N-FCNs to zebrafish embryo. As the embryos use the endogenous nutrients accumulated in yolk during their development, the delay of yolk sac absorption would affect nutrient metabolism and thus delay embryo development and hatching (Shi et al., 2022). Heart is the earliest organ to develop and function during zebrafish embryonic development (Cen et al., 2020). Commonly, the heart of embryo basically develops and beats regularly after 48 hpf, so the changes in heart rate and the pericardial region, are important indexes to evaluate cardiotoxicity (Duan et al., 2021). In this study, FCNs and N-FCNs reduce the heart rate of zebrafish, thus resulting in decreased BF. In addition, apparent PE also observed in embryos exposed to LC10 of N-FCNs. The PE and blood circulation disorders prove that FCNs and N-FCNs could cause cardiotoxicity to zebrafish embryo/larvae.

These results indicate that the toxicities of FCNs to zebrafish are mainly cardiovascular toxicity and developmental toxicity. The toxicity of NPs to organism can be ascribed to many aspects. Firstly, the previous report (Shi et al., 2022) demonstrates that carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) could induce gene changes in steroid and hormone biosynthesis pathways, thus leading to developmental toxicity such as delayed incubation of zebrafish embryos, increased malformation rate and multiple malformation phenotypes. Secondly, light plays an important role in zebrafish development (Ranjani et al., 2020). However, it is difficult for light to penetrate the high concentration of FCNs and N-FCNs solution, when the solution gets darker with increasing concentration of materials. Thirdly, at high concentration, the excess FCNs and N-FCNs that adhered to chorion (Fig. 8 ) will prevent gas exchange and form a hypoxic microenvironment, which can induce delay and malformation in embryos development (de Medeiros et al., 2021b). In addition, the nanostructure and nitrogen-containing groups in FCNs and N-FCNs can induce oxidative stress to embryos and larvae, thus inhibit their growth and development (Zhang et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2022; Ren et al., 2021), which will be described in detail in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative stress of FCNs and N-FCNs on zebrafish

ce:section.ce:para: MDA, derived from membrane polyunsaturated fatty acids peroxidation, is a primary indicator of membrane damage, thus its content can reflect stress tolerance of organisms (Wang et al., 2021). Under stress, excess ROS can quickly attack the macromolecules on the biofilm, resulting in the formation of MDA and the destruction of membrane structure and permeability (Song et al., 2022). From Fig. 5, FCNs and N-FCNs of increasing concentration from 200 to 800 mg/L gradually increase the MDA content in zebrafish larvae at 120 hpf. However, 50 mg/L of FCNs decrease the MDA content of larvae although there is no significant difference compared to the control. It might be due to that at high concentrations, the excessive ROS could not be immediately and effectively removed by the antioxidant defense system, and then ROS quickly attacked the macromolecular substances on the biofilm, resulting in the increasing of lipid peroxidation (Chang et al., 2020). In addition, ammonia toxicity derived from the NH4 + in N-FCNs can reduce immunity, thus increase the ROS concentration in vivo (Zhang et al., 2018). Furthermore, smaller particles of N-FCNs can cause more damages in zebrafish (Yu et al., 2022). The results indicate that FCNs are less cytotoxic to zebrafish larvae than N-FCNs as they are less easily to disrupt cell membrane.

To cope with the large amount of ROS, the antioxidant system in vivo could be activated by some CNMs (Deng et al., 2019). Here, FCNs from 50 to 800 mg/L cause gradually increasing total antioxidant capacity of larvae, and the maximum increase was 1.8 folds compared to the control (Fig. 5b). N-FCNs at 50 mg/L increase the larvae T-AOC by 26.5%, while higher concentration of N-FCNs decrease it. The results are due to the concentration and properties of FCNs and N-FCNs. On one hand, CDs have been proved to enhance enzymatic antioxidant system mainly through inducing the expression of antioxidant-related genes and improving the structural stability and activity of the antioxidant enzyme (Chen et al., 2020b; Wei et al., 2019). On the other hand, when the concentration of N-FCNs increase, the high doses of NH4 + cause additional tissue damage and oxidative stress (Guo et al., 2022), then the excess ROS will lead to the disruption of enzymes structure and function (Zhang et al., 2018). Furthermore, N-FCNs at high concentrations may strongly disturb endogenous antioxidant enzyme via the competitive inhibition of electron transfer process in zebrafish larvae (Deng et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative stress response of adult zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that fish liver is the most important organ responsible for metabolism and detoxification, and the gill is directly exposed to exogenous toxicant (Guo et al., 2022), the two tissues were dissected to study the stress responses of adult zebrafish exposed to FCNs and N-FCNs. After exposure for 96 h, 50 mg/L FCNs increase MDA contents in the gill and liver of adult zebrafish, but not significantly different compared to CT (Fig. 6a). However, MDA contents in the gill and liver are significantly increased by 71.3% and 80.9% when exposed to 50 mg/L of N-FCNs. In the gills, N-FCNs further increase MDA content by 54.2% compared with FCNs. As important comb filters, gills first contact the external environment, making them extremely vulnerable to contaminants (Huang et al., 2022). Therefore, MDA contents in gills are higher than that in liver. The result is consistent with the embryonic response, that is, N-FCNs cause higher level of MDA production, indicating that N-FCNs could induce more severe stress and ROS generation, thus induce more severe peroxidation reaction and oxidative stress than FCNs.

Increased ROS production activates the antioxidant mechanism, causing the increased production of antioxidant enzymes to scavenge ROS (Bhagat et al., 2021). SOD, CAT and POD are important antioxidant enzymes responsible for protecting cells against ROS, which are of great significance to body metabolism (Chen et al., 2022a; Song et al., 2022). SOD constitutes the first step of defense machinery against ROS toxicity, and catalyzes the conversion of the superoxide radicals to oxygen and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Then H2O2 is further reacted with POD and CAT to convert to innocuous H2O (Chen et al., 2022b). Carbon nanomaterials can activate the enzyme activities of antioxidant in vitro or in vivo, along with the large amount of induced ROS (Deng et al., 2019). From Fig. 6b, FCNs at 50 mg/L can significantly improve SOD activity in liver, thus improve its detoxification ability. However, N-FCNs don't significantly promote SOD activity in liver. Because ammonia suppress the SOD activity in gills (Guo et al., 2022), exposure to 50 mg/L of N-FCNs results in significantly lower SOD activity in gills than that in control or FCNs50 group (Fig. 6b), which reveals the oxidative damage of N-FCNs to zebrafish gills might exceed the antioxidant capacity of SOD (Song et al., 2022). Fig. 6c shows that CAT activity in gills is significantly activated by 50 mg/L of N-FCNs, compared to that exposed to FCNs or control. 50 mg/L of FCNs show no significant effect on CAT activity in both gill and liver, as same as N-FCNs on which in the liver. In Figs. 6d and 50 mg/L of FCNs significantly enhance POD activities in both gill and liver, whereas N-FCNs only enhance the POD activity in the gill. FCNs significantly increase POD activity by 41.7% in the gill than control, and N-FCNs increase it by 57.1%. Significant interactive effects between enzymes can be seen in FCNs treatment. 50 mg/L of FCNs activate the SOD activity in the liver of adult zebrafish, thus promote the conversion and elimination of ROS in cells (Fig. 6b) (Song et al., 2022). Therefore, the CAT and POD activities in liver are increased in response to the overproduction of H2O2 (Fig. 6 c,d), which indicates the well tolerance of zebrafish liver cells to oxidative stress. Overall, the effects of FCNs and N-FCNs on POD activity in each treatment are similar to that on CAT activity.

As the first organ to contact nanomaterials, the gills suffer more severe oxidative stress. Although the abnormal SOD activity indicates a redox imbalance in gills, CDs with intrinsic antioxidant enzyme-like catalytic activities have been proven effective in scavenging free radicals and thereby protecting cells against oxidative damages (Li et al., 2021; Bhagat et al., 2021). Therefore, although FCNs and N-FCNs increase ROS generation, they can also scavenge ROS and contribute to redox homeostasis in gills, and especially FCNs. This can be proved by the result that FCNs do not significantly increase MDA content (Fig. 6a). In the liver, which act as an important detoxification organ (Ren et al., 2021), both the SOD and POD activities are up-regulated when zebrafish are exposed to FCNs or N-FCNs. What's more, this promotion effect of FCNs is significant, reflecting the improved detoxification ability of liver induced by FCNs.

N-FCNs cause more serious fluctuations in MDA content and enzymes activities than FCNs in both larvae and adult zebrafish (Figs. 5 and 6). These results show that N-FCNs induce more serious inflammation in zebrafish than FCNs, which depends on the smaller particle size and nitrogen doping. In addition, higher doses of nanomaterials can also increase their toxicity to zebrafish, thus cause oxidative stress-mediated responses.

Compared with control group, an increasing number of apoptotic cells are observed in heart and tail region in the exposure groups (Fig. 7). LC10 of FCNs exposure group shows significant increases in relative fluorescence intensity in both cardiac cells and tail cells compared to control, revealing FCNs cause prominent injuries at heart and tail parts of zebrafish. Although the relative fluorescence intensity in tail is significantly enhanced by LC10 of N-FCNs, the heart injury is insignificant (Fig. 7). The results indicate that FCNs have adverse effects on tail development and cardiac function. This may be because FCNs, like other CNMs, can cause significant ultrastructural changes in myocardial tissue and induce the expression of inflammatory factors, thus leading to cardiac cell apoptosis (Shi et al., 2022).

The biodistribution characteristics of FCNs and N-FCNs affect their toxic mechanism (Batel et al., 2018). The zebrafish chorion, which is composed of glycosylated proteins, protects embryos and serves as the first barrier against nanoparticles (Chen et al., 2015). From TEM images in Fig. 8 and Fig. S9, both of FCNs and N-FCNs increase the thickness of chorion after 5 h exposure, which may inhibit gas exchange and result in the delay of hatching. Compared with LC10 (640 mg/L) of FCNs, relatively low concentrations of N-FCNs (197 mg/L) increase CPCs. In addition, the accumulation of FCNs and N-FCNs in CPCs are observed. Because the materials are easily aggregated in biological media containing salts (Falinski et al., 2019), the sample NPs in pores seem much larger than which in Fig. 1a. N-FCNs are more easily to bind with cells and enter into the pores than FCNs, because –NH2, –COOH, CO, and –CHO can interact specifically with various types of biological molecules and receptors in membranes (Khajuria et al., 2017). Besides, FCNs with more negative charge are more easily electrostatically repulsed by the negatively charged chorion surface (Falinski et al., 2019).

The nano-size, surface effects and oxygen-rich functional groups of FCNs and N-FCNs contributes materials excellent green luminescence, which makes it feasibility to determine the biodistribution of samples in zebrafish (Deng et al., 2019). Differences in the fluorescence intensity between the chorion and the inner regions of the embryo suggest that FCNs and N-FCNs have differing levels of affinity for different tissues (Fig. 9 ). FCNs and N-FCNs only accumulate on the chorion and not entry into embryos, which is different from previous reports that CDs can penetrate into embryos (Liu et al., 2020; Khajuria et al., 2017). But the result is consistent with the TEM result (Fig. 8), indicating the biosafety of FCNs and N-FCNs to embryos. For larvae, the fluorescence is evident in the gill under FCNs exposure, same as in the other report (Liu et al., 2020). This is because the permeability of organism to NPs at its early-life stages can only be feasible through the epithelial, such as gills (Chousidis et al., 2020). However, N-FCNs only exist with weak fluorescence signal in eyes of larvae, which may be due to the lower concentration of materials in solution. Overall, FCNs and N-FCNs are not easy to cross the physical barriers in embryos or larvae zebrafish, as they mainly distributed on the chorion, gills and eyes. Moreover, the fluorescence emitted of FCNs or N-FCNs in eyes or gills also validates the potential applications of using them as probes in eye-related or gill-related imaging.

The liver, gill, as well as intestinal excreta from adult zebrafish exposed to FCNs or N-FCNs were also analyzed using Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S10). The presence of FCNs or N-FCNs can be confirmed through the observed D (around 1300 cm−1) and G bands (around 1600 cm−1), which are distinctive of graphitic materials (Lu et al., 2017). Although FCNs and N-FCNs are relatively safe to zebrafish, we still need notice that ingestion of NPs by fish can cause loss of appetite, intestine damage, dysbacteriosis and inhibition of nutrient absorption, resulting in the low survival rate and growth retardation (Li et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2017). Although the accumulation of FCNs or N-FCNs within the larval zebrafish digestive system is not obvious in Fig. 9, they can be ingested by adult zebrafish through swallowing, and then transformed to other organs via blood circulation (Martínez-Álvarez et al., 2021, Batel et al., 2018). As a result, FCNs can be observed in the gill and liver of adult zebrafish, and N-FCNs in the gill (Fig. S10a). Fortunately, the presence of FCNs and N-FCNs in faeces indicate that the materials could be excreted from intestine (Fig. S10b), which will effectively reduce the long-term damage of these materials to zebrafish.

Tissue damages are often associated with oxidative stress induced by various environmental conditions and pollutants (Guo et al., 2020). To further explore the potential toxic response of FCNs and N-FCNs in organs, histopathological alterations to the liver, kidney and gill tissues of larvae at 120 hpf were analyzed. Histopathological observation of zebrafish liver in CT group shows clear contour of liver cells and uniform distribution of cytoplasm, no observed edema and vacuoles (Fig. 10 ). N-FCNs treated liver is normal as that in CT groups, while significant liver vacuolization and loosely arranged hepatocytes are observed in fish exposed to FCNs (Fig. 10b). The cytoplasmic vacuolization may be induced by high doses of FCNs mediated stress through the accumulation of autophagic vacuoles (Guo et al., 2022). Therefore, FCNs at high concentration cause structural damage in fish liver.

Kidney is one of the organs most affected by toxin as it is responsible for the filtration of blood residues and the absorption of salt and water (Custodio De Souza et al., 2019). However, few reports demonstrate the histopathological changes of CDs to kidney. In Fig. 10c, the renal cells in FCNs or N-FCNs treatment arrange obvious loosely and the cellular structure is blurred, especially in N-FCNs (LC10) treatment. The histopathological changes in kidney reveal the renal toxicity of FCNs and N-FCNs at LC10 to zebrafish.

Despite FCNs and N-FCNs can induce oxidative stress or absorb on zebrafish gills, no histopathological alterations are observed in the gill tissue. The structures of gills in each group are complete, the sizes of the respiratory epithelium are uniform, as well as the surfaces of the gill lamellas are smooth (Fig. 10d). Gills from both FCNs and N-FCNs treated fish exhibit no significant histopathological changes compared to control.

In conclusion, the present study demonstrates that FCNs and N-FCNs can influence the embryo hatching, early-stage development, oxidative stress, cells apoptotic and organs histopathological changes in zebrafish following a dose-dependent and time-dependent manner. The nitrogen-doped groups and smaller size amplify the ecotoxicity of N-FCNs, especially on hatching rate, survival rate, antioxidant system of zebrafish. High doses of FCNs or N-FCNs can cause cardiotoxicity, hepatotoxicity and renal toxicity to embryos or larvae zebrafish, but the materials are not easy to cross their physical barriers. Furthermore, FCNs and N-FCNs could be excreted from intestine to reduce their long-term damage to organism. Although it could not thoroughly eliminate the toxic risks of FCNs and N-FCNs in aquatic environments, the materials are “practically nontoxic” to zebrafish, and FCNs are even “relatively harmless” to embryos. As FCNs and N-FCNs possess both good biosecurity and selective fluorescence imaging in zebrafish developmental model, they are potential to be practically applied in many fields in the future.
